findings.description: yaml.load() is called without an explicit safe Loader, allowing a malicious config file to execute arbitrary Python via YAML tags (use yaml.safe_load).
Precedence is inverted versus the documented contract: env is applied before file, so the file overrides env vars, yielding file > env > defaults instead of env > file > defaults.
feature_enabled assumes feature_flags is a dict, but a config file can set it to a scalar/list/null, causing AttributeError on flags.get.
get()/require()/feature_enabled() call load() every invocation, re-reading the file from disk and re-parsing YAML plus re-scanning os.environ with no caching.
require() treats any falsy/None stored value (e.g. a key explicitly set to null in YAML) as missing and raises KeyError, conflating present-but-null with absent.
_coerce over-eagerly converts numeric-looking strings to int/float (e.g. version '1.0', zero-padded codes, '1_000', 'inf'/'nan'), silently changing intended string config values.
Env overlay lowercases and flattens key names with no support for nested keys, so nested config (e.g. individual feature flags) cannot be overridden and case-distinct keys may collide.
_read_file does not catch yaml.YAMLError, so a syntactically malformed config file raises an unhandled exception instead of degrading gracefully like the missing-file path.
